attributes.description: Alerts when Tomcat's JVM process uses more than 80% of its file descriptor limit. FD exhaustion prevents new socket connections and file operations — effectively halting request processing.
attributes.name: [Tomcat OTel] File descriptor exhaustion

investigation_guide.blob:
## Tomcat file descriptor exhaustion

### What fired
`OpenFileDescriptorCount / MaxFileDescriptorCount` exceeded 0.80 over the evaluation window.

### Why it matters
Sockets, log files, and class loading all consume file descriptors. When the JVM hits the OS-imposed limit, `Too many open files` errors cause request failures and the JVM may need to be restarted to recover. This is one of the most disruptive operational failures for Tomcat.

### Triage
1. JVM & OS Resources dashboard: confirm trend and current vs max FDs.
2. Identify leak drivers: unclosed streams, lingering sockets (check `netstat` / `ss` for CLOSE_WAIT pile-up), verbose class loading.
3. Look at request rate and connection count — scaling traffic naturally scales FDs.

### Remediation
- Raise `ulimit -n` / systemd `LimitNOFILE` and restart the JVM.
- Identify and fix FD leaks (unclosed `InputStream`, `Socket`, etc.).
- Reduce keep-alive timeouts to accelerate socket reclamation.

### Tuning
- 80% warning is conservative; some deployments alert at 70% to leave more time for operator response.

esqlQuery.esql:
FROM metrics-tomcat.otel-*
| WHERE java_lang_OperatingSystem_OpenFileDescriptorCount IS NOT NULL
    AND java_lang_OperatingSystem_MaxFileDescriptorCount IS NOT NULL
| STATS
    open_fds = MAX(java_lang_OperatingSystem_OpenFileDescriptorCount),
    max_fds = MAX(java_lang_OperatingSystem_MaxFileDescriptorCount)
  BY host.name
| WHERE max_fds > 0
| EVAL fd_pct = ROUND(open_fds / max_fds * 100.0, 2)
// File descriptor exhaustion prevents new socket connections and file operations
// Adjust threshold based on your FD limits (ulimit -n)
| WHERE fd_pct > 80.0
| SORT fd_pct DESC
